title: Genesis 13
subtitle: Abram and Lot Separate; the Land Promise Renewed

sections.header: Verses 1–7 — Return from Egypt; Conflict Between Herdsmen

poi.name: Negev to Bethel
poi.coords: Southern Canaan back to central hills
poi.text: Abram retraces his steps from Egypt: Negev → the place between Bethel and Ai where he had first camped. Returning to the first altar is a act of covenant renewal — he calls on the name of the LORD at the same spot as before (12:8).

notes.note: MacArthur: Abraham's return from Egypt (v.1) marks a resumption of the covenant journey after the detour of ch.12. His wealth is noted (v.2) -- not as a sign of blessing through deception but as material with which he will now demonstrate covenant generosity. The conflict with Lot creates the occasion for the covenant character to be shown.

MacArthur: 'The Canaanite and Perizzite were also living in the land at that time' -- an editorial note that underscores the significance of Abraham's behaviour. The watching world of Canaan is the audience before which Abraham and Lot conduct their dispute. How the covenant family behaves in conflict matters for witness.

heb.paragraph: "Abram had become very wealthy in livestock and in silver and gold" (v.2). The covenant blessing of 12:2 is already producing material fruit — precisely the kind of flourishing that covenant faithfulness generates in the OT. The prosperity, however, creates the conflict of v.7: the land "could not support them" if they stayed together. Material blessing thus generates the occasion for the separation that will clarify each man's character — Abram's generosity vs. Lot's self-interest.

The conflict (rîb) between Abram's and Lot's herdsmen (v.7) uses a word that carries legal resonance — a dispute that requires resolution through judgment or agreement. The narrator adds that "the Canaanites and Perizzites were also living in the land at that time" — noting that the covenant people's internal dispute is being conducted in front of the watching Canaanites. Abram's generous resolution of the rîb is thus a covenantal witness as well as a family settlement.

Abram's appeal — "we are close relatives (ʾanāšîm ʾaḥîm)" (v.8) — invokes kinship obligation. The word ʾaḥ (brother) in Hebrew covers a range of relatedness from biological brother to close cousin to covenantal ally. Abram's priority of relationship over territory — offering Lot first choice of the land — models the covenant principle that the relationship matters more than the inheritance. His willingness to receive the worse portion demonstrates that he trusts the divine promise more than human negotiation.

panels.ctx: Abram's generosity to Lot is striking — as the patriarch and elder, he had every right to choose first. Instead he defers, trusting God to fulfill the promise regardless of which portion of land he receives. This is a model of faith in action: holding the promise loosely, letting God provide. Lot's choice will prove catastrophically poor.

cross.note: Trusting in the LORD rather than leaning on one's own sight — the principle Abram embodies and Lot violates.
Peter calls Lot "righteous" — he was not wicked, but his choice to live near Sodom shows the danger of choosing by sight rather than faith.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The conflict between Abram's and Lot's herdsmen is the first recorded instance of the land's inability to support both men simultaneously. Sarna: "The quarrel is not between Abram and Lot personally but between their servants — a socially important distinction. The narrator adds that 'the Canaanites and Perizzites were then living in the land' (v.7), which serves two functions: it notes that the land is occupied, making the future gift more remarkable, and it implies that the quarrel among God's people is a poor witness to their neighbours."

The parallel description of Abram's and Lot's wealth sets up the separation narrative. Sarna notes the blessing that produces conflict is itself a sign of the covenant's working. The very prosperity that requires separation is evidence of God's faithfulness to his promise.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lot's possessions have grown in parallel with Abram's — "Lot, who was moving about with Abram, also had flocks and herds and tents." Alter: "The abundance is the problem: the very blessing God has given (12:2-3) has made cohabitation impossible. The land cannot hold both of them. The blessing creates the necessity of separation — a recurring pattern in Genesis where the abundance of God's gifts generates the need for division."

The conflict between Abram's and Lot's herdsmen is immediately contextualised by the Canaanites and Perizzites present in the land. Alter notes this double exposure: the covenant-land conflict between the two Hebrews is observed by its current occupants. The promise is always complicated by the already-resident.

The necessity of separation arises from abundance, not scarcity — the blessing itself creates the conflict. Calvin: "We see that even good men are sometimes entangled in contentions, not through any fault of their own, but through the malice and perverseness of others." The quarrel begins with the servants; Abram and Lot are not personally at fault. But the separation is still necessary.

Calvin on the conflict arising from prosperity: blessing itself produced the occasion for strife. This teaches that prosperity is not always the pure good it appears — it may be the testing of character. Abram's response will show whether the covenant has shaped his character or merely his circumstances.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on the Canaanites and Perizzites "dwelling in the land" observes this is an important narrative detail: the land is occupied, not empty. The promise of land to Abraham's descendants must be understood in light of the fact that others already live there — the fulfilment will involve displacement, which the Mosaic narratives will later address theologically.

The NET note observes that the parenthetical comment about Canaanites and Perizzites heightens the tension: Abram and Lot are quarrelling over land they do not own, in front of its current inhabitants. The irony is structural.

sections.header: Verses 8–13 — Abram's Generous Offer; Lot Chooses the Plain

poi.name: Jordan Plain / Sodom region
poi.coords: Dead Sea basin; the kikkar (circle) of the Jordan
poi.text: Lot chooses the "well-watered plain of the Jordan" — the kikkar, the circular basin around the Dead Sea. "Like the garden of the LORD" and "like Egypt" are both double-edged comparisons: the paradise comparison anticipates the destruction; the Egypt comparison signals the moral danger.

poi.name: Sodom and Gomorrah
poi.text: Lot pitches his tents near Sodom (v.12) — a gradual approach that will become full urban residence by ch.19. The geography of compromise is traced in steps.

notes.note: MacArthur: Abraham's generous offer -- 'let's part company; if you go to the left, I'll go to the right' -- is the covenant character in practice. As the senior partner and the covenant heir, Abraham had the right to choose first. He surrendered that right. The generosity is possible because he trusted the divine promise, not the visible landscape.

MacArthur: Lot's choice of the well-watered Jordan plain is described immediately with the ominous editorial: 'the people of Sodom were wicked and were sinning greatly against the LORD.' The beauty of the land concealed the corruption of the culture. Lot chose by sight; the sight was deceiving.

heb.paragraph: Lot "looked up and saw that the whole plain of the Jordan was well watered" (v.10) — the same gesture (wayyiśśāʾ lôṭ ʾet-ʿênāyw) that Abraham will perform after Lot's departure (v.14) when God tells him to "look from where you are." Lot's gaze is horizontal and calculating — he sees the land "like the garden of the LORD, like the land of Egypt." Abraham's gaze will be vertical and covenantal — he sees the land as divine gift. How one looks reveals the orientation of one's heart.

The narrator's aside — "the people of Sodom were wicked and were sinning greatly against the LORD" (v.13) — is a warning embedded in the geography. Lot chooses the Jordan plain for its fertility, gravitating toward Sodom without knowing (or perhaps without heeding) what its character will mean for his family. The rightward orientation of Lot's choice (v.11 — "Lot journeyed east") echoes the east-of-Eden geography of sin and exile (3:24; 4:16; 11:2). The compass direction is morally freighted in Genesis.

The separation (pārad) of Abram and Lot (v.11) is the moment of narrative divergence: Lot's story will end in the ashes of Sodom; Abram's will proceed to the stars of the covenant promise. The same root appears in the creation narrative (1:4, 6, 7, 14, 18) where God separates (yabdēl) the elements of creation. Separation in Genesis is often the condition of new beginnings: light from darkness, waters above from waters below, Abram from his homeland, and now Abram from Lot.

panels.ctx: Lot's choice is described in Eden-like language — the Jordan Valley "like the garden of the LORD." This echoes 2:8–9, but the paradise is illusory: Sodom lurks at the end of the road. The text tells us immediately that Sodom's men were "wicked, great sinners" — information Lot apparently did not consider. He chose by sight, Abram by faith.

cross.note: Lot's choice of the well-watered Jordan plain — compared to 'the garden of the LORD' (v. 10) — proves catastrophic when Sodom is destroyed. The lush landscape becomes a byword for divine judgment.
God's promise of the land (12:7) is now practically enacted: Abram lets Lot choose first, trusting that God's promise secures his inheritance regardless of Lot's selection.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Abram's offer is a remarkable act of covenant generosity: as the elder and the recipient of the divine promise, he has priority claim to the land. Yet he gives Lot first choice. Sarna: "Abram's magnanimity is all the more striking because the promise of the entire land has just been reaffirmed (12:7). He acts from the security of the promise rather than from anxiety about the land."

Lot's choice of the Jordan plain is described in terms that echo Eden: "well-watered like the garden of the LORD, like the land of Egypt." Sarna: "The Edenic description of the Jordan plain is immediately qualified by the presence of Sodom — 'but the people of Sodom were wicked and were sinning greatly against the LORD' (v.13). Lot chooses the beautiful land and gets the corrupt city."

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lot "looked up and saw the whole plain of the Jordan" — the choice is made by sight, not by faith. Alter: "Lot's choice by sight contrasts with Abram's journey by faith (12:1 — 'to the land I will show you'). Abram went without seeing; Lot sees and chooses. The narrative implication is clear: sight-based choice in Genesis is associated with the fall (3:6 — 'the woman saw that the tree was good'), not with faith."

The narrator's editorial comment — 'the men of Sodom were wicked and were sinning greatly against the LORD' — is dropped immediately after Lot settles toward Sodom. Alter notes this is the warning voice inserted into the story. The reader knows what Lot does not know.

Abram's generous offer reflects his confidence in the divine promise. Calvin: "Abram, relying on the promise of God, gives up his right, and cheerfully allows his nephew to have the preference. He who has God for his inheritance is not anxious about a portion of the earth." The security of the unconditional promise liberates Abram from competition and anxiety.

Calvin on Lot's choice: he was carried away by the lust of the eyes, choosing the better land and leaving the worse to Abram. A single glance at Sodom's fate should teach us that the fair appearance of a place is insufficient reason to choose it when the inhabitants are wicked.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on the Jordan plain "like the garden of the LORD" observes this is the first explicit comparison to Eden in the patriarchal narratives. The note discusses whether "garden of the LORD" means Eden specifically or simply an extremely fertile landscape. The note observes the structural irony: the Eden-like plain is adjacent to Sodom, the most corrupt city in the Abraham narrative.

The NET note traces the geography: the Jordan plain east of Bethel toward Sodom was indeed notably fertile. The note discusses the identification of the five cities and the archaeological evidence for a catastrophic event in the region.

sections.header: Verses 14–18 — God Reaffirms the Promise to Abram

poi.name: Hebron / Mamre
poi.coords: Southern Canaan hill country; modern Hebron
poi.text: Abram settles at the great trees of Mamre near Hebron. This will become the primary covenant camp — the site of the three visitors (ch.18), Sarah's burial negotiations (ch.23), and the family tomb at Machpelah. Hebron is the covenant's domestic centre.

notes.note: MacArthur: Immediately after Lot's departure, God reaffirms the covenant with its most expansive territorial promise to date: all the land in every direction, as far as you can see (v.14-15). The loss of Lot occasions the renewal of the promise. God's covenant purpose expands precisely when human circumstances contract.

MacArthur: Abraham built an altar at Hebron (Mamre) -- worship marks each significant stage of the covenant journey. The altars at Shechem (12:7), Bethel (12:8), and Hebron (13:18) are the punctuation marks of Abraham's progressive possession of the land through worship. He worships before he fully possesses.

heb.paragraph: God's instruction after Lot's departure — "Go, walk through the length and breadth of the land" (v.17) — is a covenant land-grant formula. Walking through territory was an ancient legal act of possession-taking. The command hithallek ("walk about in it") uses the same Hithpael stem as the "walking with God" of Enoch (5:22, 24) and Noah (6:9) — Abram's walking through the land is simultaneously a legal claim and a spiritual act of covenant obedience.

heb.gloss: north and south and east and west
heb.paragraph: God's instruction to look "north and south, east and west" (v.14) and "the land you see I will give to you and your offspring forever" is a four-directional land-grant covering the entire visual field. The gesture is comprehensive and symbolic — the entire compass of the covenant land falls within the scope of God's promise. The same four-directional formula appears in the tribal allotments of later Israel.

"I will make your offspring like the dust of the earth" (v.16) — the ʿāpar (dust) image is drawn from the creation of Adam (2:7: "formed from the dust of the ground"). The promise of innumerable descendants is thus grounded in the creation metaphor of humanity's materiality — as uncountable as the particles of the earth itself. The same promise will be elaborated with the stars of heaven (15:5) and the sand of the seashore (22:17), each image emphasising a different dimension of the covenant's scope.

cross.note: The land promise is formalized in covenant: "To your offspring I give this land, from the river of Egypt to the great river, the river Euphrates."
Abraham "lived in the land of promise, as in a foreign land, living in tents" — the promise was real but not yet possessed, requiring faith.
Paul identifies the "offspring" as singular — Christ — connecting this land promise to its ultimate fulfillment in the new creation.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: After Lot's departure, God reaffirms the land promise to Abram — now in the most expansive terms yet: "all the land that you see I will give to you and your offspring forever... as numerous as the dust of the earth." Sarna: "The reaffirmation after separation is a pattern in the Abraham narrative: each loss or limitation is followed by a divine promise of greater scope. Abram gives up the Jordan plain; God gives him the whole land."

Abram settles at the great trees of Mamre near Hebron and builds an altar. Sarna notes the pattern of altar-building: at Shechem (12:7), Bethel (12:8), and now Hebron (13:18). The altars mark the stages of Abram's journey through the promised land — a spiritual claiming of the land through worship before any legal or military possession.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The divine command "lift up your eyes and look" deliberately echoes Lot's looking in v.10. Alter: "God invites Abram to look — the same act that led Lot to choose the better land by sight. But what God shows Abram is the whole land, not just the well-watered plain. The faith-based 'looking' that God invites is more expansive than the sight-based choosing that Lot exercised."

The divine speech after Lot's departure fills the vacuum Lot's leaving created. Alter notes the rhetorical effect: loss of one companion occasions renewal of the greatest promise. God fills the space the human departure emptied.

The divine reaffirmation after Lot's departure shows that God orchestrated the separation for Abram's benefit. Calvin: "God, at length, rewards his servant Abram for his patience and moderation. The more cheerfully any one, in obedience to God, foregoes his own right, the more liberally will God recompense him." The surrender of the Jordan plain opens the promise to its fullest scope.

Calvin: God commands Abram to travel through the whole land as through his own inheritance, sustained by the divine promise alone. Though he had not yet one foot of it legally, he was to possess it by faith.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on "all the land that you see I will give to you and your offspring forever" discusses the nature of the land promise: (1) Is "forever" (leolam) absolute or conditional? (2) Does "offspring" (zera) refer to the nation Israel, to the Messiah, or to both? The note observes that Paul reads zera as singular (= Christ) in Galatians 3:16, while also acknowledging the corporate meaning (Israel) in its original context. The promise is both-and.

The NET note on 'like the dust of the earth' traces the three seed-comparisons: dust (13:16), stars (15:5), and sand (22:17). Each emphasises the covenant's staggering scope by comparison to something uncountable.

ppl.name: Abram
ppl.role: Patriarch; model of generosity and faith
ppl.text: By surrendering the first choice to Lot, Abram demonstrates the counter-intuitive economics of faith: release what you can see and trust what God has promised. The promise is immediately renewed after Lot departs (v.14).

ppl.name: Lot
ppl.text: Lot's choice is tragic in slow motion — he moves toward Sodom by stages: the valley (v.11), among the cities (v.12), then to Sodom itself (13:12 → 14:12 → 19:1, sitting in the gate). Each chapter he is deeper in.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"Lot looked around and saw that the whole plain of the Jordan toward Zoar was well watered, like the garden of the LORD, like the land of Egypt."ESV"Lot lifted up his eyes and saw that the Jordan Valley was well watered everywhere like the garden of the LORD, like the land of Egypt."KJV"And Lot lifted up his eyes, and beheld all the plain of Jordan, that it was well watered every where...even as the garden of the LORD."NASB"Lot lifted up his eyes and saw all the valley of the Jordan, that it was well watered everywhere...like the garden of the LORD, like the land of Egypt."

src.title: Josephus, Antiquities 1.8.1–2
src.quote: Josephus notes that the region of Sodom was exceptionally fertile before divine judgment, and that traces of divine fire could still be seen in his day (1st c. AD) — possibly referring to the sulfur-rich terrain around the Dead Sea.
src.note: Shows the text was read geographically/historically in the 1st century.

rec.text: Augustine uses the Abram/Lot separation to illustrate the two cities: Abram represents the City of God (ordered by love of God), Lot drifts toward the earthly city (ordered by self-interest).
Chrysostom praised Abram's yielding to Lot as a supreme act of virtue: "He gave up the whole earth to his nephew" — despite being the patriarch with prior claim.

rows.label: The Generous Offer: Covenant Character Displayed
rows.text: The elder gives the choice to the younger. The generosity is possible because Abram trusts the divine promise will hold regardless of which land he takes. Covenant faith enables the generous gesture.

rows.label: Land Renewed After Separation
rows.text: The divine reaffirmation comes immediately after Lot's departure -- filling the relational vacuum with the most expansive statement of the land promise to date. The loss of Lot occasions the renewal of the greatest promise.

hebtext.note: "Abram had become very wealthy in livestock and in silver and gold" (v.2). The covenant blessing of 12:2 is already producing material fruit — precisely the kind of flourishing that covenant faithfulness generates in the OT. The prosperity, however, creates the conflict of v.7: the land "could not support them" if they stayed together. Material blessing thus generates the occasion for the separation that will clarify each man's character — Abram's generosity vs. Lot's self-interest.

The conflict (rîb) between Abram's and Lot's herdsmen (v.7) uses a word that carries legal resonance — a dispute that requires resolution through judgment or agreement. The narrator adds that "the Canaanites and Perizzites were also living in the land at that time" — noting that the covenant people's internal dispute is being conducted in front of the watching Canaanites. Abram's generous resolution of the rîb is thus a covenantal witness as well as a family settlement.

Abram's appeal — "we are close relatives (ʾanāšîm ʾaḥîm)" (v.8) — invokes kinship obligation. The word ʾaḥ (brother) in Hebrew covers a range of relatedness from biological brother to close cousin to covenantal ally. Abram's priority of relationship over territory — offering Lot first choice of the land — models the covenant principle that the relationship matters more than the inheritance. His willingness to receive the worse portion demonstrates that he trusts the divine promise more than human negotiation.

Lot "looked up and saw that the whole plain of the Jordan was well watered" (v.10) — the same gesture (wayyiśśāʾ lôṭ ʾet-ʿênāyw) that Abraham will perform after Lot's departure (v.14) when God tells him to "look from where you are." Lot's gaze is horizontal and calculating — he sees the land "like the garden of the LORD, like the land of Egypt." Abraham's gaze will be vertical and covenantal — he sees the land as divine gift. How one looks reveals the orientation of one's heart.

The narrator's aside — "the people of Sodom were wicked and were sinning greatly against the LORD" (v.13) — is a warning embedded in the geography. Lot chooses the Jordan plain for its fertility, gravitating toward Sodom without knowing (or perhaps without heeding) what its character will mean for his family. The rightward orientation of Lot's choice (v.11 — "Lot journeyed east") echoes the east-of-Eden geography of sin and exile (3:24; 4:16; 11:2). The compass direction is morally freighted in Genesis.

The separation (pārad) of Abram and Lot (v.11) is the moment of narrative divergence: Lot's story will end in the ashes of Sodom; Abram's will proceed to the stars of the covenant promise. The same root appears in the creation narrative (1:4, 6, 7, 14, 18) where God separates (yabdēl) the elements of creation. Separation in Genesis is often the condition of new beginnings: light from darkness, waters above from waters below, Abram from his homeland, and now Abram from Lot.

God's instruction after Lot's departure — "Go, walk through the length and breadth of the land" (v.17) — is a covenant land-grant formula. Walking through territory was an ancient legal act of possession-taking. The command hithallek ("walk about in it") uses the same Hithpael stem as the "walking with God" of Enoch (5:22, 24) and Noah (6:9) — Abram's walking through the land is simultaneously a legal claim and a spiritual act of covenant obedience.

hebtext.gloss: north and south and east and west
hebtext.note: God's instruction to look "north and south, east and west" (v.14) and "the land you see I will give to you and your offspring forever" is a four-directional land-grant covering the entire visual field. The gesture is comprehensive and symbolic — the entire compass of the covenant land falls within the scope of God's promise. The same four-directional formula appears in the tribal allotments of later Israel.

"I will make your offspring like the dust of the earth" (v.16) — the ʿāpar (dust) image is drawn from the creation of Adam (2:7: "formed from the dust of the ground"). The promise of innumerable descendants is thus grounded in the creation metaphor of humanity's materiality — as uncountable as the particles of the earth itself. The same promise will be elaborated with the stars of heaven (15:5) and the sand of the seashore (22:17), each image emphasising a different dimension of the covenant's scope.

tx.title: Description of the Jordan plain
tx.content: MT describes the Jordan valley as 'like the garden of the LORD, like the land of Egypt, as you go toward Zoar.' The LXX translates faithfully but adds a minor clarification about the geographic direction. The SP agrees with MT.
tx.note: The reference to 'the garden of the LORD' is significant as a rare pre-Eden-narrative echo; textual consistency across witnesses confirms its antiquity.

tx.title: Mamre — place name stability
tx.content: MT consistently identifies Abram's settlement at 'the oaks of Mamre' (ʾêlônê mamrēʾ). LXX renders this as 'the oak' (singular, τὴν δρῦν), possibly reflecting a known cult site with a single sacred tree. Josephus also references a single tree at Mamre.
tx.note: The singular/plural oak variant may reflect different stages of the site's sacred landscape. Both readings point to Mamre as a well-known cultic location.

positions.name: Righteous alien
positions.argument: Peter explicitly calls Lot "a righteous man who was distressed by the depraved conduct of the lawless." Lot's righteousness is apostolically attested even if his choices were unwise.

positions.name: Progressively compromised
positions.argument: The narrative traces a downward trajectory: Lot chooses the Jordan plain (ch.13), moves near Sodom (13:12), lives in Sodom (14:12), is at the city gate (19:1 — a position of civic standing). Each step represents deeper integration.
